title: SEGMENT 7 — Whole-organ recellularization: where the ghosts hit a wall

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Whole-organ recellularization: where the ghosts hit a wall
The 2008 rat heart launched what I'll call the recellularization decade.
Between roughly 2008 and 2018, dozens of labs around the world raced to extend the ghost-organ trick to other organs and, eventually, to human-scale organs.
The progression went something like this.
Rat hearts.
Rat livers.
Rat kidneys.
Rat lungs.
Then pig versions of all of those — pigs are anatomically similar to humans, so a pig liver scaffold is, in shape and size, a reasonable model for a human liver.
Then human cadaveric organs, harvested from deceased donors whose organs were not transplantable, used as scaffold material.
The decellularization protocols got better.
The scaffolds got more uniform.
The mechanical preservation of the matrix got more reliable.
And in every case — heart, liver, kidney, lung — the recellularization step has been the wall.
Take the liver as an example.
The liver is a metabolically extraordinary organ.

It has, depending on how you count, between five and twenty distinct cell types — hepatocytes, which do most of the metabolic work; cholangiocytes, which line the bile ducts; stellate cells, which store vitamin A and produce extracellular matrix; Kupffer cells, the resident macrophages; sinusoidal endothelial cells, which line the unusual fenestrated capillaries; portal fibroblasts, and more.

These cells are arranged in a very precise architecture — the so-called liver lobule, a roughly hexagonal unit with hepatocytes in plates radiating outward from a central vein, separated by sinusoidal capillaries, with bile ducts running along the lobule edges.
A liver works because of that architecture, not just because it contains hepatocytes.
When you decellularize a pig liver, you get a beautiful scaffold that retains the gross lobular architecture and the full vascular tree.
When you reseed it with cells — typically a mixture of primary hepatocytes and other liver cells — the cells go in, attach to the matrix, and start functioning.
But they don't reconstitute the lobular architecture in detail.
They populate the scaffold unevenly.
Hepatocytes that should be in the periportal zone end up in the centrilobular zone, or vice versa.
The intricate cell-cell relationships that give the real liver its zonation of metabolic functions — different metabolism in different parts of the lobule — don't reestablish themselves.
The result is a piece of tissue that looks, grossly, like a liver, and performs maybe a few percent of normal liver function in vitro.
Promising.
Far from clinically useful.
The kidney has been even harder.
The kidney is, mechanically, one of the most complex organs in the body.

Its functional unit, the nephron, includes glomerular capillaries, a Bowman's capsule, a proximal tubule, a loop of Henle, a distal tubule, and a collecting duct, each with distinct cell types and distinct architectures.
Each human kidney has about a million nephrons.

Decellularization preserves the basic vascular tree, but reseeding cells in a pattern that reconstitutes nephron architecture across a whole-kidney scaffold has so far been beyond the reach of any laboratory.
The best kidney recellularization attempts produce scaffolds with islands of properly differentiated tubular tissue separated by acellular regions.
Some urine-like filtrate has been produced in some preparations.
No one has gotten close to a functional engineered kidney.
The lung sits somewhere between liver and kidney in difficulty.

Decellularized rat lungs reseeded with epithelial and endothelial cells have been kept alive in bioreactors for a few days and have shown some gas exchange when ventilated, but they degrade quickly and have not been transplantable as functional organs.
The pig and human lung scaffolds have been generated and characterized, but the recellularization step remains in early development.
The heart — where the field started — has had the most progress and is in some ways the most tantalizing case.
The Ott group and others have continued to refine the technique.
With induced pluripotent stem cells differentiated into cardiomyocytes, you can repopulate a decellularized heart scaffold with patient-derived cells in principle.
Some recent work has demonstrated reseeded pig heart scaffolds that generate measurable contractile force, though still far below physiological levels.
The fully recellularized, patient-specific, transplantable bioartificial heart remains a research goal, not a clinical reality.
Maybe a decade away.
Maybe more.
The pattern across all of these efforts is similar, and it points to a fundamental issue.
Decellularization gives you the architectural scaffold.
It does not give you the developmental instructions.

A real heart, or liver, or kidney is built during embryonic development by a complex cascade of signaling events that direct cells to migrate to particular positions, differentiate into particular fates, and form particular relationships with neighbors.
When you take adult or stem-cell-derived cells and dump them into an empty scaffold, you're asking them to skip all of that development and just "figure it out.
" They do their best.
They attach.
They survive.
They do some of the right things.
But they don't reconstitute the full functional architecture of a real organ, because that architecture was the product of a developmental program, and you've bypassed the program.
The current consensus in the field is that decellularization is a powerful research platform but probably not, on its own, the route to clinical organs.

It's likely to be one component of a hybrid approach — a decellularized scaffold combined with bioprinting to lay down cells in correct spatial patterns, combined with developmental signaling cues delivered in proper temporal order, combined with bioreactor maturation that mimics in vivo conditions.
The ghost organ is one ingredient.
The full recipe is going to need more.
